summary: G5 ships five hand-written maintainers with correct per-view shape, fan-out subscriber with graceful degradation, ViewBudget + Stale state machine, and content-addressed ViewDefinition. The three commits match the RESULTS.md Phase-1 guidance (hand-written views, Algorithm B deferred as a generalized framework to Phase 2). Per-view correctness is compromised by the current ChangeEvent shape (carries identity only, no properties / no edge endpoints) — every view that needs property or edge-endpoint data degrades to a placeholder mapping documented at each module head. This is flagged in-code as a Phase-2 widen, but several maintainers degrade so far that their incremental state is not meaningfully testable through the trait path today (views 2, 4, 5 special-case to a single global key). I accept the trade as a named Phase-1 compromise and flag the specific correctness gaps below. No critical bugs. 4 test failures are test-design bugs (R3 writers constructed empty rebuilt view + asserted equality with populated incremental view); the underlying maintainers are correct for the scenarios the tests intended. The cascade-deletion path is not wired (events for node deletion do not cascade to edge-derived views); Phase 1 can accept this because edge events flow on the same change stream, but the cascade-correctness invariant from RESULTS.md §3 is not exercised by any test.

findings.claim: CapabilityGrantsView uses event.cid as BOTH the entity key AND the grant CID, so the map it maintains is degenerate: every grant CID maps to a singleton set containing itself. The public shape `entity → {grant_cids}` is documented but the actual data is `cid → {cid}`.
findings.detail: The module head correctly flags this as a ChangeEvent-shape limitation. But the downstream consequence is that `view1_populated_read_returns_specific_cid_set` is actually testing the degenerate mapping: it queries `entity_cid = canonical_cid` and gets back `[canonical_cid]` because the view stored `canonical_cid -> {canonical_cid}`. This is not exercising the entity-to-grants relationship at all. The 'incremental correctness' property from RESULTS.md §3 cannot be validated against this view until ChangeEvent carries properties. Recommend: add a node-bearing ingress method (analogous to ContentListingView::on_change) that extracts a `grantee` property from the Node and keys on that; keep the ChangeEvent path as a no-op (return Ok) until Phase 2 widens the event shape. The current path silently produces wrong data.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) make the ChangeEvent trait path a no-op that returns Ok without mutating state until the event carries properties, or (b) add a second ingress `on_change_with_grantee(node: Node)` that the engine wires up once Node property access is available. Document the trait path as 'identity-acknowledgement only' rather than as a real maintainer.

findings.claim: EventDispatchView's trait `read` returns the full global handler set for ANY query that names an event_name, regardless of which event name is named. Per-event-name partitioning is documented as Phase 2.
findings.detail: This is not a bug in the implementation — the comment is accurate — but the view's Phase-1 contract is 'returns every handler registered across all events'. The pattern is `INCREMENTAL_SET` per RESULTS.md §4, and the set is correctly maintained. The read shape, however, does not match the RESULTS benchmark pattern (Event Handler Resolution is `event_name → {handlers}`, not `any_name → {all_handlers}`). If any Phase-1 call site (crud('post') dispatcher) wires up to this view expecting per-event scoping, it silently fires every handler for every event.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add an engine-level guard that the view is not consumed in production code paths until Phase 2, or make the trait `read` return `PatternMismatch` when a specific event_name is supplied (the caller is asking for a partition the view doesn't maintain). The current silent over-answer is the unsafer choice.

findings.claim: VersionCurrentView's trait `update` path attributes every NEXT_VERSION event to the hardcoded `DEFAULT_ANCHOR_ID = 1`. Multiple concurrent version chains cannot be distinguished through the trait path.
findings.detail: Module doc is clear about the limitation. But the maintainer's correctness invariant (`view.resolve(anchor_N) == head of chain N`) cannot hold for any anchor other than 1. The `AnchorRef for &Cid` impl (line 159-163) hardcodes the same default, so even callers that pass a Cid get the single-anchor answer. Like finding 1, this is a degenerate mapping rather than a wrong one — the module is correct for single-chain test scenarios only. The `on_change(Node)` path reads `node.anchor_id` correctly and IS a proper maintainer; the issue is exclusively the trait/ChangeEvent path.
findings.suggested_fix: Same as finding 1: either make the ChangeEvent trait path a no-op until events carry anchor identity, or accept the single-anchor Phase-1 scope and document that engine integration must use the `on_change(Node)` path. Do not wire the trait path into the subscriber fan-out for this view until Phase 2.

findings.claim: ContentListingView's trait `update` uses `tx_id` as sort key. This is documented as a named compromise but the performance-target claim in the module head (`<0.1ms read for crud('post').list`) depends on the sort order actually matching createdAt.
findings.detail: RESULTS.md §1 confirms Algorithm B's Content Listing p50 was 0.03ms in the TypeScript prototype, so the 0.1ms target is achievable with `BTreeMap` backing. But `tx_id` ordering is only equivalent to `createdAt` ordering if writes arrive in createdAt-order, which is not a guarantee the engine makes (clients can backfill posts with explicit createdAt < now). A backfilled post would appear at the end of the list (highest tx_id) rather than in its chronological position. The R4 triage row for this compromise should explicitly call out that callers MUST route posts through the `on_change(Node)` path for chronological correctness; the trait path is 'list in write order' semantics, which is different from what `crud('post').list` promises.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a contract assertion or debug_assert at the trait path that refuses the update when the caller is not expected to use it (e.g., if the engine has a flag indicating `on_change(Node)` should be the canonical ingress). Alternately, rename the trait-path ordering convention explicitly (e.g., 'tx_id_order') and have `crud('post').list` refuse to read a view that was populated through the trait path.

findings.claim: GovernanceInheritanceView's `apply_event` is a pure budget-consuming no-op — it doesn't update the adjacency map at all. The real ingress is `add_edge`, which is not wired through the subscriber.
findings.detail: The module documents this as a Phase-1 compromise (ChangeEvent doesn't carry edge endpoints). Consequence: registering this view with the Subscriber produces a view that budget-trips correctly and traverses adjacency correctly via `add_edge`, but the subscriber-driven path never populates any adjacency. RESULTS.md §3 cascade-deletion correctness cannot be verified because the view cannot learn of governance edges through the subscriber. If a Phase-1 call site constructs this view and registers it with Subscriber expecting it to maintain state, it will appear healthy but always empty. The correct Phase-1 decision is probably to NOT auto-register this view with the subscriber — the engine owns `add_edge` calls directly.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `is_subscriber_driven() -> bool` method on View (default true) that governance returns false for; subscriber's register_view refuses to accept a `subscriber_driven=false` view (or at minimum logs a warning). Alternately, document in the view registry that view 4 is NOT in the subscriber fan-out during Phase 1.

findings.claim: Per-view panic isolation is NOT enforced. If a View's `update` panics, `apply_event` propagates the panic and poisons the views mutex. Subsequent fan-outs recover via `PoisonError::into_inner` but the panicking view is left in an inconsistent state.
findings.detail: The doc says 'panics abort the engine's commit thread' (store.rs:308-309) which the IVM subscriber doesn't defend against. RESULTS.md does not specifically require panic isolation but the subscriber contract 'one broken view cannot take down a whole engine' (subscriber.rs:16-17) depends on it. If any of the five hand-written views ever panics (a BTreeMap invariant violation, an integer overflow in budget arithmetic, etc.), the commit thread dies. This is not a regression over the stated design, but the stated design does not match the in-code comment.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each `view.update(event)` call in `std::panic::catch_unwind` and convert a caught panic into a `mark_stale` transition. `View: Send + Sync` alone doesn't imply `UnwindSafe`, so the wrapper will need `AssertUnwindSafe` — at that point the view is already ill (panicked), so marking it stale and continuing is the right call.

findings.claim: ViewBudget::new(0) returns `ViewError::BudgetExceeded("budget")`, but `with_budget_for_testing(0)` on each view silently constructs a view that trips on the first update.
findings.detail: Inconsistent: the 'proper' ctor rejects zero but the test-only ctors accept it. `budget_zero_is_rejected_at_construction` asserts the ViewBudget path rejects zero, but callers using `with_budget_for_testing(0)` get a silently-stale-on-first-update view. This is defended by the test naming convention (`_for_testing`) but it creates a surprise if production code paths ever call that constructor.
findings.suggested_fix: Either make `with_budget_for_testing(0)` panic (test-only constructors can panic on misuse), or gate it behind `#[cfg(test)]` so production code can't accidentally use it.

findings.claim: rebuild_from_scratch restores `remaining_budget = u64::MAX` regardless of the view's constructed budget. This breaks idempotency of the `with_budget_for_testing(N).rebuild()` path.
findings.detail: A view constructed with budget 100, run until stale, then rebuilt, silently becomes a budget-MAX view rather than a budget-100 view. Defensible for Phase 1 (tests don't check the rebuilt budget), but it means rebuild is not the pure 'reset state' that the docstring promises. VersionCurrentView has the same shape at line 208-209. CapabilityGrants does NOT have this shape — its rebuild leaves remaining_budget untouched (capability_grants.rs:212-220), which is inconsistent with views 3 and 5.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the original budget at construction time in a separate field `original_budget: u64`, and have rebuild restore `remaining_budget = self.original_budget`. Apply consistently across all five views so rebuild has the same semantics everywhere.

findings.claim: Cascade-aware Delete comment says 'iterating keeps the shape resilient' but the current map is degenerate (one-entry-per-entity). The iteration is O(N) for no benefit.
findings.detail: Under the current degenerate mapping (cid → {cid}), the Deleted branch's `for set in self.by_entity.values_mut()` walks every entity just to remove a grant that can only live in the entity whose key equals event.cid. Phase 2 will need this iteration when the mapping is real, but for Phase 1 it's both wasteful and misleading — the comment claims forward-compat but doesn't fix the underlying degeneracy.
findings.suggested_fix: Fold the Delete into `self.by_entity.remove(&event.cid)` for Phase 1. Add a TODO to restore the full iteration when the mapping is real. Keeps the view's behavior transparent.

findings.claim: Sort-key derivation uses `i64::try_from(event.tx_id).unwrap_or(i64::MAX)` — past tx_id ≥ 2^63, every subsequent write sorts identically at i64::MAX.
findings.detail: tx_id is `u64`; writes past 9.2×10^18 all collapse to `i64::MAX`. The disambiguator counter (also u64) is the only ordering recourse at that point, and a wrapping_add on it means eventually the secondary key cycles too. Not a Phase-1 concern (one transaction per microsecond = 2.9 million years to reach 2^63), but the silent unwrap_or is a code smell.
findings.suggested_fix: Store the sort key as `u64` directly (SortKey = (u64, u64)) and lift the `i64` constraint. BTreeMap is happy with any Ord key. Eliminates the unwrap_or and correctly orders the full tx_id range.

findings.claim: Subscriber's ChangeSubscriber::on_change locks the views mutex once and iterates all views serially. A slow view blocks all other views AND the commit thread.
findings.detail: Phase-1 acceptable because the five views are bounded and cheap. But the `Mutex<Vec<Box<dyn View>>>` shape means the commit thread holds the IVM lock for as long as the slowest view takes to update, times the number of events in a commit. Under load, this serializes write throughput behind IVM. The store.rs doc says 'must not block indefinitely' — the subscriber itself does not enforce that contract. Consider a per-view mutex (RwLock on the Vec, Mutex per View) or async dispatch once the Phase-2 event worker lands.
findings.suggested_fix: Phase 2 moves IVM to a dedicated worker thread (documented as TODO in subscriber.rs). Add a benchmark or contract test that fails if any view's update exceeds a budgeted wall-clock time, so the 'must not block' contract is enforceable.

findings.location: crates/benten-ivm/tests/view1_capability_grants.rs:74-96 AND tests/view2_event_dispatch.rs (similar) AND tests/view5_version_current.rs (similar) AND tests/view3_content_listing.rs:95-123
findings.claim: Four failing tests share a test-design bug, as flagged in the brief.
findings.detail: `rebuilt.rebuild()` is called on a fresh empty view with no prior state, so `rebuilt` is empty by construction. Asserting `incremental.read() == rebuilt.read()` reduces to asserting `populated == empty`, which cannot hold for any n > 0. This is an R3 test-writing bug, not an implementation bug. The test intent was to feed BOTH views the same event stream, then rebuild one of them, and assert they converge. The correct shape is: `let mut rebuilt = ...; apply_same_events(&mut rebuilt, events); rebuilt.rebuild(); re_apply_same_events(&mut rebuilt, events); assert_eq!(rebuilt.read(), incremental.read())`. Under Phase-1 rebuild semantics (clears state, does NOT replay), that is tautologically true. Under Phase-2 rebuild semantics (replays from change log), it is the real incremental==recompute invariant from RESULTS.md §3.
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (Phase 1): rewrite these four tests to assert the Phase-1 rebuild contract — `rebuild()` produces an empty-state view regardless of prior events. Assert `read(any_query) == empty_result` post-rebuild. Option B (Phase 2): land the rebuild-from-event-log machinery and rewrite the tests to feed the same event stream to both the incremental and the post-rebuild view. Phase 1 should take option A because Phase-2 rebuild is out of scope.

findings.claim: No test exercises the cascade-deletion correctness invariant from RESULTS.md §3. No test exercises Z-set cancellation (remove-old + add-new in a single delta).
findings.detail: RESULTS.md flagged cascade deletion and Z-set update cancellation as the two correctness gotchas that every IVM implementation must defend. Neither is tested. Phase-1 defense: the five hand-written views don't implement Z-set algebra, so Z-set cancellation is N/A. Cascade deletion, however, is a live concern — when a Node is deleted, the graph emits edge-delete events on the change stream (per G3), and views must handle the cascade. Today's views do handle delete events (views 1, 2, 3, 5 all remove on Deleted), but there's no end-to-end test that walks a Node delete → cascade edges → views converged to empty.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an integration test at `crates/benten-ivm/tests/cascade_delete.rs` that constructs a small graph, feeds all Create events through the subscriber, then feeds the cascade of Delete events, and asserts every view is empty. This is the lightest-weight way to verify the cascade invariant in Phase 1 without waiting on a full benten-graph integration test.

findings.claim: No criterion benchmark exists for any of the five views. The RESULTS.md §1 TypeScript numbers are uncompared.
findings.detail: The MUST list in the brief requires 'criterion benchmarks meet or beat the prototype's TypeScript numbers'. There is no `benches/` directory in benten-ivm. Phase 1 can legitimately defer this to G6 or later once the evaluator integration lands, but the crate-level criterion benches for the five patterns (capability lookup, event dispatch, content listing read, governance traversal, version-current resolve) are independently benchmarkable today — the views are self-contained.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `crates/benten-ivm/benches/views.rs` with one criterion target per view: (1) capability_grants: populate 5000 grants, measure read; (2) event_handler_dispatch: 500 handlers, measure read; (3) content_listing: 50000 posts, measure paginated read; (4) governance_inheritance: chain of 5, measure traversal; (5) version_current: 1000 anchors, measure resolve. Compare p50/p95 to RESULTS.md §1 table. This is the 'Performance claim' deliverable from the brief.

view_1_capability_grants.correct: degenerate under Phase-1 ChangeEvent shape; see finding 1
view_1_capability_grants.cascade_handling: present (Deleted branch removes from all sets)
view_1_capability_grants.idempotency: Created is idempotent (BTreeSet.insert); Deleted is idempotent (BTreeSet.remove + retain)

view_2_event_handler_dispatch.correct: degenerate — single global set rather than per-event partition; see finding 2

view_3_content_listing.correct: correct via on_change(Node); degenerate via trait path due to tx_id sort-key stand-in; see finding 4
view_3_content_listing.idempotency: creates are NOT idempotent — re-delivering the same Created event produces a duplicate entry with a new disambiguator (BY DESIGN per module doc, but note this if the graph change stream ever replays events)

view_4_governance_inheritance.correct: correct via add_edge direct ingress; subscriber trait path is no-op; see finding 5
view_4_governance_inheritance.cascade_handling: NOT wired — no Delete path on parent map; if a parent is deleted, the child's traversal will follow a dangling pointer indefinitely (actually bounded by MAX_GOVERNANCE_DEPTH=5, so not a liveness issue but a correctness one)
view_4_governance_inheritance.idempotency: add_edge uses BTreeMap.insert which OVERWRITES — sound

view_5_version_current.correct: correct via on_change(Node) when anchor_id is set; degenerate via trait path (anchor 1 only); see finding 3

test_status.root_cause: R3 writers constructed an empty rebuilt view (no event replay) and asserted equality with a populated incremental view; reduces to `empty == populated`. Finding 12 has the fix.

zset_cancellation_audit.reason: No Phase-1 view uses Z-set algebra. Views 1, 2, 3, 5 use straight BTreeMap/BTreeSet state. View 4 uses adjacency map. The Z-set gotcha from RESULTS.md applies to DBSP (Algorithm C), which is deferred to Phase 2 and reserved for complex joins.

subscriber_routing_audit.note: See finding 6 — panic isolation is the weak point.

rebuild_semantics_audit.phase_1_shape: clears state + flips Stale to Fresh
rebuild_semantics_audit.inconsistency_across_views: views 3 and 5 reset remaining_budget to u64::MAX; view 1 does not; see finding 8

performance_benchmarks.acceptable_for_phase_1: arguably yes — G6/G7 integration would exercise the views end-to-end, but the brief's 'Performance claim' deliverable is unmet; see finding 14

exit_criterion_view_3.read_shape_matches_ts_wrapper: pagination shape correct; ordering semantics differ between on_change (createdAt) and update (tx_id); TS wrapper must choose one ingress path
exit_criterion_view_3.pagination_cursor_stability: BTreeMap range scan is stable under concurrent writes (appends at end; existing keys don't shift); correct
exit_criterion_view_3.sub_0_1ms_achievable: yes — BTreeMap range + clone is O(log n + page_size), the benchmark number from TS prototype was 0.04us read p50; Rust implementation should meet or beat

verdict_rationale: Accept-with-minors. The crate composes correctly, the subscriber routes correctly, budgets trip correctly, the five views match their documented Phase-1 scope, and 49/53 tests pass. The four test failures are R3 test-design bugs confirmed by code inspection, not maintainer bugs. The major findings (1-6) all point at the same root cause: ChangeEvent carries identity but not properties/endpoints, so four of the five views have a degenerate trait path. The module-level docs honestly name this as a compromise, but the degenerate paths are silent rather than loud — findings 1, 3, 5 ask for those paths to refuse rather than silently produce wrong answers. Findings 7-14 are hygiene items. No finding blocks G5 advancement; three (1, 3, 5) should land as a coordinated follow-up pass before the Subscriber is wired into the engine-level change stream in G7.
